questions.q: What does IAS mean?
questions.a: I, A, and S are the three interest dimensions that stand out in this result. Their order describes this result ranking; it is not a fixed identity.

questions.q: Does this tell me which career I should choose?
questions.a: No. It points to work activities that may be worth exploring or validating first. Real choices also depend on ability, experience, opportunity, values, entry barriers, and market conditions.

questions.q: Does a score of 80 mean I scored higher than 80% of people?
questions.a: No. The current 0-100 score is a within-form interest-strength conversion. It is not a population percentile and it is not an ability score.

questions.q: Does low E mean I cannot start a business, sell, or manage?
questions.a: No. Low E only means high-persuasion, high-exposure, and high-competition activities may not be the most natural interest entry point. Capability still depends on skills, experience, and real opportunities.

questions.q: Does low R mean I cannot do technical or hands-on work?
questions.a: No. Low R does not mean clumsy or unable to learn technical work. It means tools, field work, and physical operation may not be the most natural interest entry point right now.

questions.q: Is 140Q more accurate?
questions.a: Do not read it that way. 140Q observes tasks, environments, and role responsibilities in more context. It does not overturn 60Q and it does not compare raw scores across forms.

questions.q: What if I disagree with the result?
questions.a: Record the disagreement, check answer quality, near ties, and activity imagination, then use low-risk experiments or a same-form retest to validate. Feedback does not change scores.

questions.q: Why are occupation examples not expanded into a direct list?
questions.a: Occupation names are easy to misread as conclusions. FermatMind shows activities and tasks first. Occupation names are only context examples and must keep source status visible.

questions.q: Can I share the report with a teacher, counselor, or friend?
questions.a: Start with the safe share version. When a full report includes personal interest structure or feedback history, you should decide whether to show it.

questions.q: Can an employer use this for screening?
questions.a: No. This report must not be used for recruiting, admission, promotion, elimination, or qualification decisions.

questions.q: Why does the result page include Action Lab?
questions.a: Career interests need real-world validation. Action Lab supports low-risk experiments and does not generate career conclusions.

questions.q: What if a retest looks different?
questions.a: First check whether it used the same form, answer quality, near ties, and your state at the time. Do not chart raw score gains or drops across different forms.

questions.q: Can a Holland Code change over time?
questions.a: Interest structure often has some stability, but experience, opportunity, and environment can affect expression. Treat retesting as a clue about change, not a win-or-lose result.

questions.q: How is this different from an ability test?
questions.a: RIASEC looks at interest in activities. It does not decide whether you are already good at them. Ability needs other evidence.

questions.q: Why not just tell me the single path I should take?
questions.a: A direct conclusion can hide real-world constraints. A steadier approach is to read activities, tasks, environments, and roles first, then test small next steps.

questions.q: If I already have a target occupation, should I still read RIASEC?
questions.a: Yes. Put the target into aspiration calibration and read which activities overlap with current interest clues, and which real-world parts still need validation.

questions.q: Can I show the PDF to a counselor?
questions.a: Yes, but once a PDF is forwarded the platform cannot remotely recall it. A counselor discussion version should be used only with your permission.

questions.q: Why does the result page not show every score detail by default?
questions.a: The default view shows levels and explanations to reduce false precision. Expanded views can show more evidence when appropriate.

questions.q: Is a higher score always better?
questions.a: No. High interest can be a more natural energy entry point, but it can also carry practical costs. Low interest is not a defect.

questions.q: Can I use this report to choose a college major?
questions.a: It can be an exploration input, but not the only basis. Major decisions also depend on course content, ability foundation, portfolio, resources, location, and long-term commitment.

v5_editorial_note: English FAQ lifecycle draft covers user misunderstanding paths, share/PDF/history, and non-recommendation boundaries.
